20   Let f be a polynomial with real coefficients.

     The integral Ip,q where p < q is defined by

                                                  q

                                         Ip,q = ∫ ( f ( x )) − ( f ( | x| ) ) dx
                                                            2              2

                                                  p


     Which of the following statements must be true?

           1   Ip,q = 0 only if 0 < p

           2   f ′(x) < 0 for all x only if Ip,q < 0 for all p < q < 0

           3   Ip,q > 0 only if p < 0

     A   none of them

     B   1 only

     C   2 only

     D   3 only

     E   1 and 2 only

     F   1 and 3 only

     G   2 and 3 only

     H   1, 2 and 3




                                                       22

stem.rawText:
Let f be a polynomial with real coefficients.

     The integral Ip,q where p < q is defined by

                                                  q

                                         Ip,q = ∫ ( f ( x )) − ( f ( | x| ) ) dx
                                                            2              2

                                                  p


     Which of the following statements must be true?

           1   Ip,q = 0 only if 0 < p

           2   f ′(x) < 0 for all x only if Ip,q < 0 for all p < q < 0

           3   Ip,q > 0 only if p < 0

solution.rawText:
2
We note first that when x ≥ 0, f (|x|) = f (x), and so for x ≥ 0, the integrand is just f (x)        −
     2
f (x) = 0. When x < 0, though, the integrand could take any value.

1 We present a complicated approach first.
  There seems to be no obvious reason why this must be true; one could imagine a polynomial
  for which the values of the integrand are positive for some negative values of x and negative
  for others, so the positive parts and negative parts cancel out making the integral zero even
  though p < 0. Here is one example: consider a quartic f (x) that looks roughly like this:

                                                             y




                                                                       x
                                                    x0   0       −x0
                                                2          2
  For negative values of x close to x = 0, f (x) > f (|x|) . But once x < x0 , we have
        2         2
   f (x) < f (|x|) as f (|x|) > f (x). We can therefore take q = 0 and find some p < x0 for
  which Ip,q = 0.
  A much simpler approach is that we can take p = 0, q > 0 and then Ip,q = 0, so this statement
  is not true. Were the question to have said 0 ≤ p instead of 0 < p, though, we would not have
  been able to use this simpler approach.
2 We could take f (x) = 1 − x, with f 0 (x) = −1 < 0 for all x.

                                                     y




                                                                       x
                                                0




            2        2
  Then f (x) > f (|x|) for all x < 0, so Ip,q > 0 for all p < q < 0.
3 We observedR at the start of this question that the integrand is zero for all x ≥ 0. So if p ≥ 0,
                q
  then Ip,q = p 0 dx = 0. The contrapositive of this statement is: if Ip,q 6= 0, then p < 0. The
  given statement (Ip,q > 0 only if p < 0, which is the same as saying: if Ip,q > 0, then p < 0)
  is a special case of the more general result and is therefore true.

The correct answer is therefore option D, 3 only.
